If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").

188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.

245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.

269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair}
273	  % NOTE (iter-005 review): \lean{} corrected. The monolithic name `ofShortExact_twist` was
274	  % never created; the prover (iter-005) realized this lemma's content as a cluster: the twist
275	  % family `horseshoeτ`, the cocycle identity `horseshoeτ_cocycle`, the recursion kernel
276	  % `twistPair`, and the augmentation `horseshoeβ` (with helpers horseshoeβ₁/horseshoeH/
277	  % horseshoeτZero/ιC0). Planner: consider splitting this block into per-declaration sub-blocks
278	  % for cleaner 1-to-1 coverage; see recommendations.md (session_5).
279	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
280	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
281	  \[
282	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
283	  \]
284	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
285	  identity
286	  \[
287	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
288	  \]
289	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
290	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
291	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
292	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
293	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
294	\end{lemma}
295	
296	\begin{proof}
297	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
298	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
299	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
300	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
301	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
302	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
303	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
304	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
305	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
306	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
307	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
308	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
309	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
310	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
311	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
312	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
313	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
314	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
315	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
316	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
317	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
318	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
319	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
320	\end{proof}

489	\section{The dimension-shift step}
490	
491	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
492	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
493	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
494	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
495	complex-level homology long exact sequence
496	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
497	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We isolate the result as the kernel
498	sub-lemma below.
499	
500	\begin{lemma}[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
501	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
502	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
503	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
504	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
505	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
506	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
507	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
508	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
509	  % between abelian categories and assume
510	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
511	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
512	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
513	  % \begin{enumerate}
514	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
515	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
516	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
517	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
518	  % \end{enumerate}
519	  % In each of the three cases
520	  % $$
521	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
522	  % $$
523	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
524	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
525	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
526	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
527	  \[
528	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
529	  \]
530	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
531	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then:
532	  \begin{enumerate}
533	    \item the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of right-derived functors
534	      furnish isomorphisms
535	      \[
536	        (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
537	        \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1;
538	      \]
539	    \item in the lowest degree there is an isomorphism
540	      \[
541	        (R^{1} G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr),
542	      \]
543	      i.e.\ \((R^1 G)(A)\) is the cokernel of the map \(G(J) \to G(Z)\) induced by
544	      the surjection \(J \twoheadrightarrow Z\).
545	  \end{enumerate}
546	\end{lemma}
547	
548	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
549	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
550	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
551	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
552	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
553	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
554	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
555	\begin{proof}
556	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
557	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
558	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
559	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
560	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
561	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
562	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
563	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
564	
565	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
566	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
567	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
568	  complexes
569	  \[
570	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
571	  \]
572	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
573	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
574	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
575	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
576	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
577	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
578	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
579	  \[
580	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
581	      \longrightarrow 0
582	  \]
583	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
584	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
585	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
586	  \[
587	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
588	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
589	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
590	  \]
591	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
592	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
593	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
594	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
595	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
596	  sequence of right-derived functors
597	  \[
598	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
599	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
600	  \]
601	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
602	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
603	  \[
604	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
605	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
606	  \]
607	
608	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
609	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
610	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
611	  \[
612	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
613	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
614	  \]
615	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
616	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism; this is part (1).
617	  In the lowest degree the segment
618	  \[
619	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A)
620	      \to \underbrace{(R^1 G)(J)}_{=0}
621	  \]
622	  is exact with vanishing right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is
623	  the image of \(G(J) \to G(Z)\); hence \(\delta^0\) identifies
624	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr)\) with \((R^1 G)(A)\), which is
625	  part (2).
626	\end{proof}

662	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
663	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
664	  Let
665	  \[
666	    0 \longrightarrow A
667	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
668	      \longrightarrow \cdots
669	  \]
670	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
671	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
672	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
673	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
674	  \[
675	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
676	  \]
677	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
678	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
679	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
680	  object.
681	\end{theorem}

695	% $$

697	% $$

699	\begin{proof}
700	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
701	    lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
702	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
703	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
704	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, with the long exact sequence at each
705	  step supplied by the kernel Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}. Decompose the
706	  resolution into the
707	  short exact sequences obtained from its cosyzygies. Put \(Z^0 := A\) and, for
708	  \(m \geq 1\), let \(Z^m := \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr) =
709	  \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr)\) be the \(m\)-th cosyzygy; exactness
710	  of the resolution gives, for every \(m \geq 0\), a short exact sequence
711	  \[
712	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
713	      \longrightarrow 0, \tag{$S_m$}
714	  \]
715	  with \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\) and
716	  the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis.
717	
718	  \emph{Base degrees.} Applying the left-exact functor \(G\) to the resolution and
719	  using Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self} (\(R^0 G \cong G\)), the complex
720	  \(G(J^\bullet)\) has, in degrees \(0\) and \(1\),
721	  \[
722	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) = \ker\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)\bigr) \cong G(A)
723	      = (R^0 G)(A),
724	  \]
725	  the last identification because \(G\) left exact carries
726	  \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\) to a left-exact sequence
727	  \(0 \to G(A) \to G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) and \(Z^1 \hookrightarrow J^1\) makes
728	  \(\ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)) = G(A)\). For degree one,
729	  the dimension-shift Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to the
730	  sequence \((S_0)\), \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to Z^1 \to 0\), gives
731	  \[
732	    (R^1 G)(A) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\bigr)
733	      \;\cong\; H^1\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
734	  \]
735	  the second isomorphism because \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1) \hookrightarrow G(J^1)\) and the
736	  image of \(G(Z^1)\) in \(G(J^1)\) is exactly \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2))\) (again by
737	  left-exactness of \(G\) applied to \((S_1)\)), so the cokernel of
738	  \(G(J^0) \to G(Z^1)\) coincides with \(\ker(G(J^1) \to G(J^2)) /
739	  \operatorname{im}(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^1(G(J^\bullet))\).
740	
741	  \emph{Induction (the shift).} For \(n \geq 1\), iterate the connecting
742	  isomorphism. The sequences \((S_0), (S_1), \ldots, (S_{n-1})\) all have acyclic
743	  middle terms, so Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(1) yields, for each
744	  \(1 \le m \le n-1\) and each \(k \geq 1\),
745	  \[
746	    (R^{k} G)(Z^{m+1}) \;\cong\; (R^{k+1} G)(Z^{m}),
747	  \]
748	  and applying part (1) to \((S_0)\) gives
749	  \((R^{k} G)(Z^{1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) for \(k \geq 1\). Composing these
750	  isomorphisms down the staircase
751	  \[
752	    (R^n G)(A)
753	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
754	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
755	      \;\cong\; \cdots
756	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1}),
757	  \]
758	  reduces the computation of \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor, which
759	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}(2) applied to \((S_{n-1})\),
760	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), evaluates as
761	  \[
762	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
763	  \]
764	  Finally, as in the degree-one base case, left-exactness of \(G\) applied to
765	  \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\), identifies the inclusion
766	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) with \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\), whence
767	  \[
768	    \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr)
769	      \;\cong\;
770	      \frac{\ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr)}
771	           {\operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr)}
772	      \;=\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr).
773	  \]
774	  Combining the staircase with this last identification gives
775	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for all \(n \geq 1\); together with the
776	  base degrees this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
777	
778	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the kernel
779	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
780	  horseshoe lift (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) and the complex-level
781	  homology long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) --- on
782	  the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\)
783	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). The injective objects supplied
784	  by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
785	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
786	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
787	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
788	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
789	\end{proof}

193	    rwa [Injective.comp_factorThru] at h3

200	construction. Given two cochain complexes `K`, `L` and a degreewise family
201	`τ n : L.X n ⟶ K.X (n+1)` satisfying the cocycle identity
202	`L.d n (n+1) ≫ τ (n+1) = -(τ n ≫ K.d (n+1) (n+2))`, the biproduct objects `K.X n ⊞ L.X n` with the
203	twisted matrix differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]` form a cochain complex `twistedBiprod τ hτ`,
204	the coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod` and projection `twistedBiprod ⟶ L` are chain maps, and every
205	degree is the canonical split short exact sequence of the biproduct.

207	This is exactly the content of the blueprint sub-lemmas `lem:horseshoe_dComp` (the differential
208	squares to zero) and `lem:horseshoe_chainMap` (the coprojection/projection are chain maps and the
209	degrees split); it is isolated here free of injectivity because the only inputs are the cocycle
210	identity and the biproduct structure. The horseshoe specialises it to `K = I_A.cocomplex`,
211	`L = I_C.cocomplex` once the twist `τ` has been produced by injectivity. -/

238	lemma twistedBiprodD_comp (n : ℕ) :
239	    twistedBiprodD τ n ≫ twistedBiprodD τ (n + 1) = 0 := by
240	  apply biprod.hom_ext
241	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_fst, Preadditive.comp_add,
242	      twistedBiprodD_fst_assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
243	    rw [Preadditive.add_comp, Category.assoc, Category.assoc, HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d,
244	      comp_zero, zero_add, ← Preadditive.comp_add, hτ n, add_neg_cancel, comp_zero]
245	  · simp only [Category.assoc, twistedBiprodD_snd, twistedBiprodD_snd_assoc, zero_comp]
246	    rw [HomologicalComplex.d_comp_d, comp_zero]

248	/-- The twisted biproduct cochain complex with differential `[[d_K, τ], [0, d_L]]`. -/
249	noncomputable def twistedBiprod : CochainComplex 𝒜 ℕ :=
250	  CochainComplex.of (fun n => K.X n ⊞ L.X n) (twistedBiprodD τ) (twistedBiprodD_comp τ hτ)

253	lemma twistedBiprod_X (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).X n = (K.X n ⊞ L.X n) := rfl

255	@[simp]
256	lemma twistedBiprod_d (n : ℕ) : (twistedBiprod τ hτ).d n (n + 1) = twistedBiprodD τ n :=
257	  CochainComplex.of_d _ _ _ _

259	/-- The coprojection `K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ`, degreewise `biprod.inl`. -/
260	noncomputable def twistedBiprodInl : K ⟶ twistedBiprod τ hτ where
261	  f n := biprod.inl
262	  comm' i j hij := by
263	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
264	    simp only [twistedBiprod_d]
265	    apply biprod.hom_ext <;> simp

267	/-- The projection `twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L`, degreewise `biprod.snd`. -/
268	noncomputable def twistedBiprodSnd : twistedBiprod τ hτ ⟶ L where
269	  f n := biprod.snd
270	  comm' i j hij := by
271	    obtain rfl : i + 1 = j := hij
272	    simp [twistedBiprod_d]
